coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot capped gold nanoflowers for electrochemiluminescent aptasensor of thrombin
coredata.dc:creator: You, Xu
Lin, Wei
Wu, Huan
Dong, Yongqiang
Chi, Yuwu
coredata.dc:description: A simple method has been developed to synthesize carbon dot capped gold nanoflower (CD/AuNF) nanohybrids. In principle, anodic electrochemiluminescent (ECL) carbon dots (CDs) with a lot of reductive functional groups were used to react with oxidative hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate in the presence of trisodium citrate. The obtained globular CD/AuNF nanohybrids had uniform sizes of 50–60 nm. The CD/AuNF nanohybrids were composed of gold nanoparticle aggregates in the centers and CDs on the surfaces. The easy self-assembly with some bio-molecules of AuNFs and the unique ECL activities of the CDs, made the obtained CD/AuNF nanohybrids promising in biosensing. For instance, a novel ECL aptasensor for thrombin (TB) detection has been developed based on the CD/AuNF nanohybrids. The aptasensor showed good selectivity towards TB. Furthermore, it presented a wide linear response range from 0.5 nM to 40 nM and a low detection limit (S/N = 3) of 0.08 nM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of preparation of CD/AuNF nanohybrids (a) and fabrication of ECL aptasensor (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CD/AuNF nanohybrids (a); TEM (b) and HRTEM (c) images of the obtained CD/AuNF nanohybrids; ECL response of CD/AuNF nanohybrids in the absence and presence of 50 μM H2O2; The scan rate of potential is 100 mV/s (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

EIS of each surface modification step performed in 0.1 M KNO3 containing 20.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. Bare GCE (a), GCE/AuNPs (b), GCE/AuNPs/Apt1 (c), GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME (d), GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME/TB (e), GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME/TB/Apt2 (f) and GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME/TB/Apt2/CD/AuNF nanohybrids (g). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ECL profiles of the aptasensor towards TB standards with different concentrations (a). Calibration plot of the ECL intensity against the concentration of TB for the aptasensor under the optimization conditions (b). Specificity for the determination of TB using the proposed ECL aptasensor (concentrations of all the biomolecules are 1 nM, all data was the average of five parallel tests) (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dot capped gold nanoflowers for electrochemiluminescent aptasensor of thrombin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fujian 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple method has been developed to synthesize carbon dot capped gold nanoflower (CD/AuNF) nanohybrids. In principle, anodic electrochemiluminescent (ECL) carbon dots (CDs) with a lot of reductive functional groups were used to react with oxidative hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate in the presence of trisodium citrate. The obtained globular CD/AuNF nanohybrids had uniform sizes of 50–60 nm. The CD/AuNF nanohybrids were composed of gold nanoparticle aggregates in the centers and CDs on the surfaces. The easy self-assembly with some bio-molecules of AuNFs and the unique ECL activities of the CDs, made the obtained CD/AuNF nanohybrids promising in biosensing. For instance, a novel ECL aptasensor for thrombin (TB) detection has been developed based on the CD/AuNF nanohybrids. The aptasensor showed good selectivity towards TB. Furthermore, it presented a wide linear response range from 0.5 nM to 40 nM and a low detection limit (S/N = 3) of 0.08 nM.

A simple method has been developed to synthesize carbon dot/gold nanoflower (CD/AuNF) nanohybrids, which were further applied in a novel ECL aptasensor of thrombin detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of emerging luminescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit outstanding photoluminescence (PL), chemiluminescence (CL) and excellent electrochemiluminescence (ECL) properties benefiting from the quantum confinement and edge effects [1–3]. Furthermore, the unique properties including low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and robust chemical and photonic inert make CDs an ideal candidate to replace the semiconductor quantum dots [4,5]. Therefore, broad interest has been attracted on the fascinating materials. Based on the unique luminescent properties, CDs have been applied in many fields, such as chemical sensing, bio-sensing and bio-imaging [5–7]. In particular, the ECL sensing systems based on CDs usually show high sensitivity and good selectivity [8–10]. As ECL luminophores, CDs usually exhibit strong cathodic ECL emission in the presence of peroxydisulfate, sulphite and l-cysteine [11,12]. However, some particularly modified CDs can produce strong anodic ECL signal in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [10,13]. The anodic ECL signal of CDs may supply a high stability of ECL sensor for bio-marker detection due to the fact that the relatively low applied potential can avoid the damage of the modified electrodes and bio-molecules.

In most case, CDs need to be hybridized with some other nanomaterials before the bio-sensing applications, for signal amplification and/or conjugating with bio-molecules [8,10,14–17]. Nanosized gold materials have been proved to be suitable precursors to hybridize with ECL luminophores for sensing due to the excellent biocompatibility and ease of functionalization [18,19]. Herein, a simple insitu method is developed to synthesize CD capped gold nanoflower (CD/AuNF) nanohybrids, which show strong anodic ECL signal in the presence of H2O2 and are promising in biosensing.

Thrombin (TB) is an important protein in molecular biology, whose activity and concentration are crucial in physiological and pathological conditions [20]. Developing simple, rapid strategies for the detection of TB with high sensitivity and selectivity is apparently of great significance in clinical applications. It has been well studied that two aptamers can target TB with high affinity and specificity [21,22]. In this paper, a novel ECL aptasensor for TB detection is developed based on the as-synthesized CD/AuNF nanohybrids.

Vulcan XC-72 carbon black was purchased from Cabot Corporation (USA). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), lysosome (Lys), l-cysteine (L-Cys) and l-glutamate (L-Glu), trisodium citrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and hydrazine hydrate (N2H4) were obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Thrombin (TB) was from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Aptamers and 2-mercaptoethanol (2-ME, 98.0%) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co. (Shanghai, China). Human IgG (IgG) were obtained from Biocell Biotechnol. Co., Ltd (Zhengzhou, China). All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of various pH values were prepared by mixing appropriate volumes of 0.1 M H3PO4 and 5 M NaOH stock solutions. Doubly distilled (DI) water was used throughout the experiment. The base sequences are as following (from Sangon, Shanghai):
Apt 1: 5′-SH-(CH2)6-GGTTGGTGTGGTTGG-3′.
Apt 2: 5′-SH-(CH2)6-AGTCCGTGGTAGGGCAGGTTGGGGTGACT-3′.

CDs were synthesized according to a previously reported method [13,23]. In brief, 5 g XC-72 carbon black was refluxed with 500 mL 8 M HNO3 at 130 °C for 24 h. Then stopped the heating and cooled the suspension to room temperature. After that, the suspension was centrifuged to collect the supernatant, which was subsequently dried at 150 °C to remove the residual acid and water. Subsequently, the obtained orange powder (about 1 g) was dissolved in 300 mL 0.3 M N2H4 solution, which was followed by a reflux at 65 °C for 24 h. The solution was then dried under vacuum to remove water and the redundant N2H4. Finally, some black CD powder was obtained. The CDs exhibit strong ECL signal in the presence of H2O2 due to the large quantities of formed luminol-like units, which has been discussed elsewhere [13].

Fig. 1 a shows the synthesis procedures of the CD/AuNF nanohybrids. In principle, the CD/AuNF nanohybrids were synthesized through the redox reaction between the CDs and HAuCl4, using sodium citrate as the additional stabilizer. In a typical experiment, 5 mg of pre-synthesized anodic ECL active CDs and 3.7 mg of sodium citrate were dissolved in 25 mL of DI water at room temperature, followed by the addition of 25 μL of 0.05 M HAuCl4 under stirring. HAuCl4 was first reduced by sodium citrate to generate citrate-protected gold seeds. Then CDs as reducing and shape-directing agents continued to reduce HAuCl4 so that they could be directed to the periphery of gold seeds to form gold nanoflowers. The stirring was kept for 3 h. Then the obtained materials were well washed with DI water by centrifugation, and re-dispersed in 5 mL DI water. The obtained CD/AuNF hybrid solution was stored at 4 °C for further use and characterization.

AuNPs were synthesized according to a widely used method [24]. In brief, 40 mg HAuCl4 was dissolved in 100 mL DI water which was subsequently heated to boiling, accompanying with violent stirring. Then, 0.11 g trisodium citrate dissolved in 10 mL DI water was added rapidly to the boiling solution. The boiling was continued for about half an hour, until the color of solution turned ruby, indicating the formation of AuNPs. The stirring was kept for another 1 h until the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. Finally, the AuNPs were washed by centrifugation for 3 times. The sediment was re-dissolved with 50 mL water and stored at 4 °C.

UV/vis spectra were characterized by a UV/vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements were performed on an electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN). Electrochemical and ECL measurements were carried out on an ECL detection system (MPI-E, Remex Electronic Instrument Co. Ltd., Xi'an, China) equipped with a three-electrode system (a modified GCE as the working electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode containing 3 M KCl solution). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were measured by an AutoLab μAUTIII.FRA2.v electrochemical workstation (Eco Chemie, The Netherlands) in a solution of 0.1 M KNO3 containing 20 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6].

As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the as-prepared CD/AuNF nanohybrids were modified on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to construct an ECL aptasensor for the detection of TB. In detail, a disk GCE with a diameter of 3 mm was successively polished with 0.05 μm Al2O3 and washed ultrasonically in water for about 1 min before use. First, 4 μL of the as-prepared AuNP solution was dropped on the polished GCE, then dried naturally at room temperature to obtain an AuNP-modified GCE (GCE/AuNPs). Then, 4 μL of 1 nM Apt1 was dropped on the modified electrode surface and dried naturally. The binding of Apt1 could be achieved via the covalent bond formed between the thiol group of Apt1 and AuNPs (GCE/AuNPs/Apt1). Subsequently, 4 μL of 100 mM 2-ME was dropped on the modified electrode surface and dried naturally, blocking the unconjugated surface and active sites (GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME). Afterwards, 4 μL TB of different concentrations was dropped on the modified electrode and dried naturally. Thus, TB was immobilized on the modified electrode surface due to the specific recognition affinity reaction between TB and Apt1 (GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME/TB). After that, 4 μL of 1 nM Apt2 was subsequently dropped on the modified electrode and naturally dried. Apt2 was conjugated with TB via the specific recognition affinity reaction (GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME/TB/Apt2). Finally, 4 μL of the obtained CD/AuNF nanohybrid solution was dropped on the modified electrode surface and naturally dried. The CD/AuNF nanohybrids were immobilized on the electrode via the covalent bond formed between the thiol group of Apt2 and Au atom of CD/AuNF hybrids (GCE/AuNPs/Apt1/2-ME/TB/Apt2/CD-AuNF nanohybrids). After washing, the electrode was ready for ECL measurement. It should be noted that the modified electrode was rinsed thoroughly with PBS before every next modification step.

The UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared materials exhibits a strong absorption peak at 525 nm assigned to the typical absorption of AuNPs and a broad background absorption band of the CDs (Fig. 2 a) [12] implying the formation of AuNP-CD nanohybrids. The FTIR spectrum of the nanohybrids shows all characteristic peaks of CDs (Fig. S1). However, the relative intensity of the CO group of nanohybrids is stronger than that of CDs. Furthermore, nanohybrids present a new absorption peak of NO2 group at 1500 cm−1. The results suggest that the CDs have been oxidized by HAuCl4. In other words, the nanohybrids should be formed by the redox reaction of the CDs and the HAuCl4. TEM and HRTEM were used to characterize the sizes and morphologies of the obtained nanohybrids (Fig. 2b and c). As shown in Fig. 2b, the obtained nanohybrids are globular nanoflowers with diameters of 50–60 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2c) indicates that the nanoflowers are composed of a large amount of AuNPs, which shows marked interplanetary spacing of 0.23 nm (111). The surface of AuNFs is coated by a thin layer, which may formed by the CDs. In a word, the as synthesized materials are CD/AuNF nanohybrids.

Apparently, the morphology of the CDs is changed greatly during the reaction, aggregating from small dots (Fig. S2) into the thin layer. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate whether the CD/AuNF nanohybrids present the same anodic ECL properties. As shown in Fig. 2d, the CD/AuNF nanohybrids dispersed in solution produce a weak ECL signal at a potential higher than +0.4 V. The anodic ECL intensity is greatly enhanced by H2O2, suggesting that the CD/AuNF nanohybrids have excellent anodic ECL activity like the free CDs. In addition, the CD/AuNF nanohybrids are easily to be bio-functionalized due to the presence of AuNFs. Therefore, the CD/AuNF nanohybrids should be powerful ECL probes that may be used in biosensing. Herein, an ECL aptasensor for TB detection would be developed to evaluate the application potential of the CD/AuNF nanohybrids in biosensing.

To develop the ECL aptasensor, two important factors affecting the ECL response of the CD/AuNF nanohybrids are optimized. Firstly, the ECL activity of the CDs/AuNFs is dependent on the pH value of the solution (Fig. S3). There is nearly no detectable ECL signal when the pH value of solution is below 6. When the pH value is increased from 6 to 12, the ECL intensity increases firstly with the pH value, and reaches the maximum at pH 9, then decreases with the further increase of pH value. The pH-dependent ECL activity of the CD/AuNF nanohybrids is related to the ECL properties of the luminol-like units on the CDs, which has been discussed elsewhere [13]. Therefore, in the following ECL detection, pH 9 PBS is used as the buffer solution. Secondly, the ECL intensity is also affected by the concentration of H2O2 (Fig. S4). The ECL intensity first increases sharply with the concentration of H2O2, and reaches the maximum when the concentration of H2O2 is 2 mM, then decreases slightly as the concentration of H2O2 is further increased. Accordingly, 2 mM H2O2 is used as the coreactant of CD/AuNF nanohybrids the following aptasensor.

The surface features of the working electrode at the modification stages are characterized with EIS, which is an effective method to probe the features of surface-modified electrode. As shown in Fig. 3 , the diameter of the semicircle decreases as AuNPs are modified on the GCE, implying that the modification of AuNPs can decrease the electron transfer resistance due to the high electrical conductivity. However, as Apt1, ME, TB, Apt2, CD/AuNF nanohybrids are immobilized on the electrode surface in sequence, the diameter of the semicircle increase gradually. The results indicate that biomolecules and the ECL luminophores are successfully immobilized on the electrode surface, hindering the electron transfer.

Under the optimum conditions, the sensitivity and dynamic range of the aptasensor are evaluated. It can be seen from Fig. 4 a that the ECL intensity of the aptasensor increases with the concentration of TB. The calibration plot shows a linear relationship in the range from 0.5 nM to 40 nM (Fig. 4b). The linear equation is y = 668.46 X + 947.1, and the detection limit for TB is about 0.08 nM (S/N = 3). The analytical performances of the as-proposed ECL aptasensor in the detection of TB are comparable with those of other reported methods [25–29].

The specificity of the aptasensor is evaluated by incubating the ECL aptasensor in different potential interferences, including bovine serum albumin (BSA), human IgG (IgG), l-glutamate (L-Glu), l-cysteine (L-Cys), lysosome (Lys), adenosine triphosphate (ATP). As shown in Fig. 4c, an obviously higher signal change can be observed for the target TB than the interfering agents, suggesting a good specificity of the developed ECL aptasensor for TB.

In summary, CD/AuNF nanohybrids are synthesized for the first time. The as-prepared CD/AuNF nanohybrids are composed of AuNFs in the center and a layer of CD aggregate on the surface. The CD/AuNF nanohybrids present the unique properties of AuNFs and the anodic ECL activity of CDs. Therefore, a sensitive and selective ECL aptasensor is developed for the detection of TB based on the CD/AuNF nanohybrids.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375020, 21675027), and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No.IRT_15R11).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1 mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.024.
